skill.name: twitter-automation
skill.description: Automate Twitter/X with posting, engagement, and user management via inference.sh CLI. Apps: x/post-tweet, x/post-create (with media), x/post-like, x/post-retweet, x/dm-send, x/user-follow. Capabilities: post tweets, schedule content, like posts, retweet, send DMs, follow users, get profiles. Use for: social media automation, content scheduling, engagement bots, audience growth, X API. Triggers: twitter api, x api, tweet automation, post to twitter, twitter bot, social media automation, x automation, tweet scheduler, twitter integration, post tweet, twitter post, x post, send tweet
skill.summary: Automate Twitter/X workflows including posting, liking, retweeting, DMs, and user management via the belt CLI.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The skill wraps the legitimate inference.sh belt CLI for Twitter/X automation. All 37 external command detections are `belt` CLI invocations restricted by `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)`, which limits execution scope. The 7 network detections are documentation and image URLs pointing to inference.sh infrastructure. The 2 high-severity 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives — no cryptographic code exists in SKILL.md. No prompt injection attempts or data exfiltration patterns were found. Risk is medium due to authorized network access to Twitter/X APIs and file I/O via belt.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via belt CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill invokes the `belt` CLI extensively to make authenticated calls to Twitter/X APIs. While `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` restricts the execution scope to belt commands only (a good security practice), any compromise of the belt binary or inference.sh platform would directly affect the user's Twitter account. All 37 external command patterns detected are legitimate belt invocations for posting, liking, retweeting, sending DMs, and user management.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence of belt CLI invocations in the skill. The allowed-tools restriction limits scope, but the commands still execute externally with authenticated access to the user's Twitter account.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to inference.sh Infrastructure
medium_findings.description: The skill references URLs pointing to inference.sh documentation and cloud storage. Additionally, the belt CLI makes network requests to Twitter/X APIs on behalf of the user. The documented URLs are benign (documentation, image hosting), but the runtime behavior includes authenticated API calls to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All hardcoded URLs are to the legitimate inference.sh platform. The network risk is inherent to the Twitter automation use case rather than a security flaw.

low_findings.title: External Skill Installation Commands
low_findings.description: The skill includes `npx skills add` commands to install related skills. These are convenience references rather than auto-execution, but users should review what they install.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are documentation examples showing how to install companion skills, not auto-executed. Low risk as they are user-initiated.

dangerous_patterns.title: Belt CLI Execution with Twitter API Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple `belt app run` commands execute with the user's authenticated Twitter session. The `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` restriction provides reasonable containment, but belt has full access to the connected Twitter account.

content.value_statement: Posting tweets, managing engagement, and handling DMs on Twitter/X requires constant manual effort. This skill automates these workflows through the belt CLI, letting you schedule posts, like, retweet, and follow users without leaving your AI assistant.
actual_capabilities: Post text tweets to Twitter/X using belt CLI
Create posts with media (images and videos)
Like, retweet, and delete tweets by ID
Send direct messages to users by recipient ID
Follow users and retrieve user profiles
Chain with AI image and video generation apps for content workflows
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI installation and account authentication
Subject to Twitter/X API rate limits and platform policies
Cannot perform actions outside the belt CLI's available app set
Media uploads require pre-hosted URLs rather than direct file uploads

use_cases.title: Schedule Social Media Content
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can automate tweet posting schedules, ensuring consistent brand presence without manual intervention.

use_cases.title: AI-Generated Content Pipeline
use_cases.description: Creators can generate images or videos with AI and automatically post them to Twitter/X as part of a chained workflow.

use_cases.title: Community Engagement Bot
use_cases.description: Developers building community tools can automate liking, retweeting, and following workflows to grow audience reach.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Post a tweet saying 'Excited to announce our new product launch next week! Stay tuned for updates.'

prompt_templates.title: Generate and Post an AI Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of a futuristic city skyline at sunset and post it to Twitter with the caption 'The future is now 🌆'

prompt_templates.title: Engage with a Specific Tweet
prompt_templates.prompt: Like tweet ID 1234567890 and then follow the user who posted it.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Welcome DM
prompt_templates.prompt: Send a direct message to user ID 'abc123' saying 'Welcome to our community! Thanks for following us.'

output_examples.input: Post a tweet saying 'Hello from Claude!'
output_examples.output: The belt CLI executes the x/post-tweet app with the provided text. The tweet appears on your connected Twitter/X account. The CLI returns a confirmation with the tweet ID and URL.

output_examples.input: Generate an image of a sunset and post it to Twitter
output_examples.output: Step 1 generates the image via falai/flux-dev-lora and captures the output URL. Step 2 uses x/post-create with the image URL and caption to publish the post. The final result is a media tweet on your timeline.
The belt CLI invokes x/user-follow with the username. If the account exists, a follow action is initiated. The CLI returns a success confirmation with the target user's profile information.

best_practices: Authenticate belt CLI once with `belt login` before running multiple automation tasks
Use `belt app sample` to generate input templates before crafting custom JSON inputs
Chain AI generation apps (image, video) with x/post-create for full content pipelines
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for spam, mass following, or aggressive engagement that violates Twitter/X terms of service
Avoid sharing your belt authentication credentials or running belt commands on shared/public machines
Do not attempt to bypass Twitter/X rate limits by running rapid-fire belt commands

faq.question: What CLI does this skill use?
faq.answer: This skill uses the belt CLI from inference.sh. Install it with `npx skills add belt-sh/cli` and authenticate with `belt login`.

faq.question: Can I post images and videos to Twitter?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the x/post-create app with a media_url parameter pointing to a publicly accessible image or video file.

faq.question: Does this work with both Twitter and X?
faq.answer: Yes. The belt CLI apps target the X (formerly Twitter) API and work with any X-compatible account that is connected through inference.sh.

faq.question: Is there a rate limit?
faq.answer: Yes. Twitter/X API rate limits apply. The belt CLI returns error messages when limits are reached, and you should pace your automation accordingly.

faq.question: Can I schedule tweets for later?
faq.answer: Direct scheduling is not built in, but you can combine this skill with a cron job or scheduler to run belt commands at specified times.

faq.question: What other apps are available on belt?
faq.answer: The belt platform has 250+ apps including AI image generation, video generation, and avatar creation. Run `belt app store` to browse all available apps.
